food_subgroup: Mixed rice bowl with meat and vegetables

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002572 represents bibimbap as a general category; no pork-specific bibimbap entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKR covers bibimbap as a category; pork-specific variant not separately coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for bibimbap or Korean mixed rice dishes.

preparation_method: assembled in stone pot or bowl

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of pork bibimbap is approximately 450 g, comprising a bowl of steamed rice topped with seasoned pork, assorted blanched and stir-fried vegetables, a fried egg, and gochujang sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Bibimbap, with meat — National Institute of Agricultural Sciences (NAAS) Korean Food Composition Table, adapted with USDA SR Legacy values for pork and vegetable components
nutrient_source.organization: Rural Development Administration (RDA) Korea / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient profile derived from the Korean Food Composition Table (RDA/NAAS) entry for bibimbap with meat, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked pork loin and mixed vegetable components. Values represent a composite estimate for a standard pork bibimbap serving normalised to per-100 g basis. Energy calculated using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; mixed rice dish with protein and vegetables analogous estimate; white rice GI moderated by protein, fat, fibre, and vinegar/sauce components
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pork bibimbap. GI estimated at 54 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for mixed rice dishes with protein and vegetables. Plain cooked white rice has a GI of approximately 72; the addition of pork protein, sesame oil (fat), gochujang, and mixed vegetables (fibre) is well-documented to lower the effective GI of the composite dish to the low range (≤55). Analogous reference: mixed rice bowl with meat and vegetables, GI ~50–56, per Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. GL per serving = (54 × 18.5 × 4.50) / 100 = 44.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g; ~1395 mg per serving) from gochujang, soy sauce, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium seasoning, as a standard 450 g serving provides approximately 1395 mg sodium, representing over 60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from white rice base, partially offset by protein, fat, and dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pork bibimbap has a low estimated GI of 54 and a glycemic load of approximately 44.9 per serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor total carbohydrate intake (~83 g per serving) and consider requesting a smaller rice portion or substituting brown rice to further reduce glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from pork and egg, combined with elevated sodium from fermented condiments
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A standard serving provides approximately 4 g saturated fat and 1395 mg sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose lean pork cuts, limit gochujang quantity, and balance this meal with low-sodium foods throughout the day.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork bibimbap (돼지고기 비빔밥) is a Korean mixed rice bowl dish consisting of steamed white rice topped with seasoned and stir-fried pork, an assortment of blanched and sautéed vegetables (typically spinach, bean sprouts, carrots, zucchini, and shiitake mushrooms), a fried or soft-cooked egg, and a spoonful of gochujang (Korean fermented chilli paste), finished with sesame oil and sesame seeds. The dish is traditionally served in a heated stone pot (dolsot) that crisps the bottom layer of rice, or in a regular bowl. The name 'bibimbap' literally means 'mixed rice' in Korean, and the dish is eaten by thoroughly mixing all components together before consuming. The pork is typically marinated in soy sauce, garlic, ginger, and sesame oil before cooking, contributing umami depth and moderate fat content. Nutritionally, pork bibimbap provides a balanced macronutrient profile per 100 g: approximately 130 kcal, 6.8 g protein, 3.2 g fat, and 18.5 g carbohydrate. The dish has an estimated glycemic index of 54 (low), as the protein, fat, and fibre from vegetables moderate the glycemic response of the white rice base. Sodium content is moderate to high due to gochujang and soy sauce seasoning, making portion control relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is a culturally significant Korean staple widely available in Singapore's Korean restaurant scene.
common_user_queries: How many calories in pork bibimbap?
What is the GI of bibimbap?
Is pork bibimbap high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No exact pork bibimbap entry was found in a single approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from the Korean RDA/NAAS Food Composition Table entry for bibimbap with meat, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy component data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for mixed rice dishes with protein and vegetables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation is methodologically sound and the source data is from approved primary databases.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the Korean RDA/NAAS Food Composition Table for this dish category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. Trans fat is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for the pork and vegetable components used — no industrial trans fats are present in traditional bibimbap preparation. GI is an analogous estimate (GI 54, low category) based on University of Sydney GI Database mixed rice dish entries; no direct measurement for pork bibimbap was located. GL per serving calculated as (54 × 18.5 × 4.50) / 100 = 44.9 g. Sodium estimate reflects typical restaurant preparation with gochujang and soy sauce; home-prepared versions may have lower sodium. The dish as served at Mincheng Bibimbap (Funan, Singapore) uses a stone pot (dolsot) presentation. Halal status is not confirmed as pork is a primary ingredient; this dish is not suitable for Muslim consumers.

Added Chinese simplified and traditional names for the stone pot variant.
